claim: 80% of what agents do is stable and well-defined, but 20% keeps changing as users change, and that 20% is what breaks your business
actionable: Monitor and continuously evaluate the changing 20% of agent behavior rather than static benchmarks; use self-curating test suites from agent traces to capture drift
source_title: Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?

claim: Traditional static benchmarks and handcrafted evaluation datasets become 'eval calcification' when AI systems adapt and evolve—the benchmark no longer measures what matters
actionable: Build evals as self-optimizing systems that evolve with the application using agent traces and telemetry feedback loops instead of fixed test datasets
source_title: Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?

claim: Agents should self-curate test suites from their own execution traces; when customer behavior changes, feed those traces into agents to detect the change and auto-update tests
actionable: Instrument agent systems to capture execution traces and implement automated processes that feed trace data back into agents to regenerate relevant test cases
source_title: Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?

claim: Telemetry in the loop enables self-correcting agents: if the harness knows what's breaking and how much it costs, it can correct itself autonomously
actionable: Instrument agentic systems to emit detailed telemetry about failures and costs, then build feedback loops that allow agents to self-adjust behavior based on that signal
source_title: Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?

claim: Evals should define an end state (intent-based) rather than a right answer; the benchmark is a self-optimizing system that grows with the application
actionable: Shift from comparing outputs against fixed golden datasets to defining desired end-state outcomes and letting agents optimize toward intent
source_title: Malleable Evals: Why Are We Evaluating Adaptive Systems with Static Tests?
